intro: This interactive visualization presents the predicted BOLD response across a 20,484-vertex fsaverage5 cortical mesh, representing neural activation patterns during video perception. You can rotate and explore the three.js rendered surface to observe how specific regions of the cortex respond to the visual stimuli.
bold: The BOLD signal tracks changes in blood oxygen levels. When neural activity increases, the brain demands more energy, triggering a surge of oxygenated blood to that area. Because this blood flow surge follows neural activity with a ~2-second hemodynamic delay, fMRI measures the resulting change in magnetic properties to map which brain regions are currently active.
tribe: Developed by UC Berkeley’s Gallant Lab, TRIBE v2 is a brain-response foundation model. It predicts per-vertex BOLD activation across 20,484 fsaverage5 vertices over 100 timepoints (50 seconds at 2Hz). By processing multimodal inputs—including video, audio, and text—the model simulates neural responses to complex stimuli. This powerful tool advances neuroimaging research. The model is released under a CC-BY-NC license for non-commercial use.
fsaverage5: The fsaverage5 cortex mesh is a standard FreeSurfer template used for anatomical alignment. It features 20,484 vertices total (10,242 per hemisphere) and is available in inflated and pial variants. Due to its consistent topology, it serves as the canonical low-resolution mesh for reporting results in neuroimaging publications, facilitating easy comparison across different subjects and studies.
toddler: Your eyes see the cute kitty! They send a message to the part that helps you see. Then, a happy part of your brain gets a tiny "yay!" It makes you feel giggly and warm inside. Your brain is having a little party!
clinician: During the 20-second cat video stimulus, you would observe localized increases in BOLD signal intensity across several functional networks. In the primary visual cortex (Brodmann area 17/V1) and secondary visual areas (V2), you would see robust activation reflecting the high-contrast luminance changes and basic motion features of the video. Within the dorsal stream, specifically area MT/V5, activation would reflect the processing of the cats' directional movement. Concurrently, the superior temporal sulcus would show increased signal related to biological motion and social stimuli. Temporally, the hemodynamic response function will show a characteristic delay, with signal intensity peaking approximately 5 to 6 seconds after the video begins. Following this peak, the signal will gradually decay, returning to baseline levels by roughly 12 seconds.
researcher:
TRIBE v2 predicts the expected hemodynamic response function (HRF) across the Glasser parcellation by integrating multimodal latent representations. For a 20-second cat video, the model synthesizes visual features from the V-JEPA 2 encoder, acoustic patterns from wav2vec-BERT, and semantic context from Llama-3.2-3B. The framework maps these high-dimensional embeddings to voxel-wise BOLD signals, modeling the temporal evolution of the task-evoked response.

Predictions typically show peak activation in early sensory and associative regions: primary visual areas (V1, V2, V3) respond to transient motion and luminance, while hMT+ tracks optical flow. Higher-order regions like the superior temporal sulcus (STSdp) and fusiform face area (FFC) exhibit sustained activity driven by semantic processing of feline features and social cues. While the model captures robust inter-subject patterns of stimulus-driven activation, it must account for significant intra-subject variability arising from anatomical differences and idiosyncratic neural dynamics.
colormap: A diverging colormap like `RdBu` is ideal because it highlights deviations from a baseline. White represents zero change, while red and blue signify positive activation and deactivation, respectively. Symmetry is crucial here, as it ensures that equal magnitudes of activation and deactivation are visually balanced around the neutral center.
pipeline: The pipeline begins with a Gemma 4 E4B vision check for media gating. Next, a GPU swap transfers processing to TRIBE v2 on an RTX 5090, performing a forward pass to generate the BOLD timeseries. The GPU then swaps back to Gemma for narration. Finally, three.js renders the BOLD signal directly onto the cortex mesh. This entire workflow operates locally, ensuring complete privacy with no cloud dependency.
